name: Chromium Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Chromium. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Chromium oxide removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength with good Chromium absorption for efficient cleaning

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise Chromium surface cleaning

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient Chromium cleaning throughput

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective Chromium oxide removal

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective Chromium oxide ablation

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform Chromium surface treatment

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete Chromium oxide removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform Chromium surface cleaning

power.description: Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of reflective metals like Chromium to avoid excessive heating; starting at 200W balances efficiency and safety. Max limited to prevent substrate melting (mp ~1907°C). Source: IPG Photonics specs for Yb-fiber lasers at 1064 nm; studies in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on metal cleaning.

scan_speed.description: Effective range for contamination removal; slower for heavy oxides (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light contaminants (2000-5000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance on Chromium's high thermal conductivity. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals; case studies in Journal of Cleaner Production (2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (5-100 ns) standard for industrial cleaning to ablate contaminants with minimal HAZ; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise work but less common industrially. 20 ns recommended for Chromium to limit thermal diffusion. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019) on pulse regimes for metals.

repetition_rate.description: Typical 10-100 kHz for pulsed cleaning; higher rates increase speed but risk heat accumulation on reflective Chromium. 50 kHz recommended for efficient coverage without overheating. Trade-off: high freq accelerates process but requires monitoring. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2021) on repetition rate effects in laser ablation.

energy_density.description: Fluence range below Chromium's ablation threshold (~4 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal; damage threshold ~2-5 J/cm² depending on pulses. 1.5 J/cm² recommended to account for high reflectivity (~60% at 1064 nm). Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2017) and Surface & Coatings Technology (2022).

spot_size.description: Typical beam diameter for precise industrial cleaning; smaller (100-200 μm) for fine details, larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform Chromium surface preparation. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems; process guidelines in ILSC standards.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on Chromium without excessive exposure. Source: Industry practice in case studies from Laser Cleaning International reports and Applied Optics (2020).

overlap_ratio.description: Typical 30-80% for uniform coverage and to avoid gaps/striping; 60% recommended for even energy distribution on Chromium's conductive surface. Source: Process optimization studies in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2019).
